food_subgroup: Noodle dishes — dry-tossed egg noodles with plant-based protein and curry sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for kolo mee or dry curry kolo mee. FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0GKM (noodle-based dish) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for kolo mee or dry curry kolo mee in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for kolo mee or this specific vegan noodle preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of dry curry kolo mee with minced soy and fried yuba, approximately 350 g, as typically served at a Singapore casual dining restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from component analysis: HPB Singapore Foodo (kolo mee noodles, curry sauce), USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (textured soy protein, fried tofu skin), and FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo); USDA Agricultural Research Service; FAO/INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for dry curry kolo mee with minced soy and fried yuba exists in any single approved database. Values are estimated by component-weighted analysis: kolo mee noodles (HPB Foodo reference for wonton noodles/kolo mee), curry sauce contribution (HPB Foodo curry noodle soup base adapted for dry preparation), minced soy protein (USDA FDC SR Legacy: textured soy protein, dry, NDB 16120 scaled to cooked weight), fried yuba/tofu skin (USDA FDC SR Legacy: tofu, dried-frozen, NDB 16127 adapted). Weighted average per 100 g of finished dish calculated assuming approximate composition: 55% noodles, 20% curry sauce, 15% soy mince, 10% fried yuba.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of noodle-based dishes — analogous estimate based on egg noodles (GI ~50) from the University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for dry curry kolo mee with minced soy and fried yuba. GI estimated at 50 based on the dominant carbohydrate source being egg noodles (wheat-based, GI approximately 46-54 per University of Sydney GI Database entries for boiled egg noodles). Curry sauce, soy protein, and fried yuba contribute fat and protein which further attenuate glycemic response. GI of 50 is a conservative mid-range estimate for this mixed noodle dish. GL per serving = (50 x 24.5 x 3.5) / 100 = 43.1 g per 350 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~1680 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content from soy sauce and curry seasoning, which can significantly contribute to the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; portion reduction or requesting reduced-sodium preparation is advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load with low-GI noodles partially offset by high protein and fibre from soy mince and yuba
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI of egg noodles (GI ~50) and the protein and fibre contributions from minced soy protein and fried yuba help moderate postprandial glucose response; however, the total glycemic load per serving (~43 g) is substantial, so individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size carefully and pair with non-starchy vegetables.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat content with favourable unsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio; high sodium is a concern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish provides predominantly unsaturated fats from soy and plant-based ingredients and is cholesterol-free, which is favourable for cardiovascular health; however, the high sodium content (~1680 mg per serving) poses a risk for blood pressure management and should be considered by individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dry Curry Kolo Mee with Minced Soy and Fried Yuba is a vegan adaptation of the classic Sarawakian kolo mee, a dry-tossed egg noodle dish originating from Kuching, Malaysia, and popular in Singapore. In this plant-based version, springy wheat-based egg noodles are tossed in a fragrant dry curry sauce made from a blend of spices including turmeric, cumin, coriander, and chilli, with shallot oil providing richness and gloss. The protein components are minced soy (textured vegetable protein rehydrated and seasoned to mimic minced meat) and fried yuba (tofu skin that has been deep-fried to a golden, crispy texture). The dish is served dry — meaning the sauce clings to the noodles rather than forming a soup broth — resulting in a bold, spicy, and aromatic flavour profile with a satisfying chew from the noodles and a contrast of textures from the crispy yuba and tender soy mince. Nutritionally, this dish provides approximately 198 kcal per 100 g, with 9.2 g protein, 7.8 g fat, and 24.5 g carbohydrate. The soy protein and yuba contribute plant-based protein and dietary fibre (2.4 g per 100 g), making this a more nutritionally complete vegan noodle option compared to plain noodle dishes. The GI is estimated at 50 (low), reflecting the moderate glycemic impact of egg noodles attenuated by protein and fat. Sodium content is high (~480 mg per 100 g) due to soy sauce and curry seasoning, which is a clinical consideration for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The dish is entirely vegan, dairy-free, and cholesterol-free, making it suitable for plant-based diets, though it contains gluten and soy allergens.
common_user_queries: Is dry curry kolo mee vegan?
Calories in dry curry kolo mee
Dry curry noodles with tofu skin
High protein vegan noodle dish Singapore

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this specific dish exists in any single approved primary source database. Nutrient values are estimated by component-weighted analysis using HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, and FAO/INFOODS SEA references. GI is an analogous estimate based on egg noodle GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are well-established approved databases and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-weighted estimates; no single approved database entry exists for this exact dish. Values should be validated against laboratory analysis if this dish is to be used in clinical or regulatory contexts. 2. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as all protein sources are plant-based (soy protein and yuba are cholesterol-free by nature). 3. Vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero) as this is a fully vegan dish with no animal-derived ingredients; plant foods do not naturally contain vitamin B12. 4. Vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero) as no vitamin D-fortified ingredients are present and plant-based foods generally contain negligible vitamin D. 5. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as no hydrogenated fats are used in this plant-based preparation. 6. Vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as this micronutrient is not reliably reported across the component sources used for estimation. 7. Vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as this micronutrient is not consistently reported in the HPB Foodo and FAO/INFOODS SEA references used. 8. Folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as folate data is not consistently available across all component sources. 9. Vitamin_e_mg is -1 as vitamin E is not reported in the primary component sources used. 10. Selenium_mcg is -1 as selenium data is not available in the HPB Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA references used. 11. GI set to 50 (low) based on analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database egg noodle entries; gi_category is low per the Veritas threshold of GI <= 55 = low. 12. Sodium content is high and is the primary clinical concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. 13. This dish is served at Flavours by Saute - Funan, Singapore (field survey data source record). Price SGD 14.30.

q1_food_identity.notes: Canonical English food name is precise and descriptive. Mandatory local-language names present for Singaporean cuisine: Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi. G6 satisfied.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: All four core macros present and numeric: energy_kcal 198.0, protein_g 9.2, fat_g 7.8, carbohydrate_g 24.5. Nutrient source clearly documented as component-weighted estimate from HPB Foodo, USDA FDC SR Legacy, and FAO/INFOODS SEA. No zero values in core macros.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: All four required secondary nutrients present and numeric: dietary_fibre_g 2.4, sodium_mg 480.0, saturated_fat_g 1.4, sugar_g 2.8. Values are plausible for a curry noodle dish.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index 50 (numeric, >0), gi_category 'low' (correct per threshold <=55=low), glycemic_load_per_serving 43.1, gi_source documented with University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed journal (Diabetes Care 2008). Analogous estimate basis clearly documented.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) used as closest_parent with gap note. FoodEx2 A0GKM (noodle-based dish) used as closest_parent with gap note. SNOMED CT set to none with gap note. No codes invented. At least one defensible ontology anchor present.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters (approximately 1650 characters). Covers: what the food is, preparation method, taste and texture, cultural context (Sarawakian kolo mee origin), clinical relevance (sodium, GI, vegan), and key nutrients. common_user_queries and meal_context populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high concern, sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (moderate concern, GI and GL), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate concern, sodium and fat profile). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' used correctly.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases list contains 19 entries covering English variants, Chinese simplified and traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and romanized forms. aliases_by_language populated for all mandatory languages. All strings in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in flat aliases list. Consistency check passed.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Previous iteration produced invalid JSON (empty output from gemini engine). Rebuilt complete valid JSON from scratch.

Iteration 3: Verified all hard gates and Q1-Q8 criteria. Confirmed aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases. Confirmed zero-value justifications in notes. Confirmed GI category threshold compliance (GI 50 <= 55 = low). Confirmed all -1 sentinel values documented in notes. Confirmed ontology match_type values use only allowed strings. Output is valid JSON.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: PASS — All six hard gates satisfied. Score 8/8. Valid JSON conforming to RESULT template. Nutrient values estimated from approved component sources (HPB Foodo, USDA FDC SR Legacy, FAO/INFOODS SEA) with transparent methodology. GI analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database. Mandatory multilingual names present for Singaporean cuisine (Chinese simplified and traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). All zero and -1 values documented in notes. Ontology uses only allowed match_type values. Aliases consistent between flat list and aliases_by_language. All three health conditions present with clinical guidance.
